Page | 4  

 
 
 1. Introduction 

This document provides basic information on using the Nimbus-7 Temperature Humidity 
Infrared Radiometer (THIR) Level-1 Cloud Data for SBUV/TOMS (BCLT) product. 

1.1 Data Product Description 

The Nimbus-7 Temperature Humidity Infrared Radiometer (THIR) Level-2 Cloud Data for 
SBUV/TOMS product contains total cloud amounts; radiances at three cloud altitudes: low 
(below 2 km), middle (2 to 7 km depending on latitude), and high (above the middle cloud 
layer); cirrus and deep convective clouds; and mean and RMS deviations of cloud and surface 
radiances. Data are averaged orbit by orbit onto each of the Nimbus 7 TOMS IFOV, which vary 
from 50 km x 50 km at nadir to 200 km x 200 km at the edges, as well as SBUV 180 km x 180 km 
IFOVs. BCLT is a new version of CLT, with improved cloud estimation. 

Each file contains one day of data (~14 orbits per day). Spatial coverage is global. The data are 
available 1978/10/31 (day of year 304) through 1984/10/27 (day of year 301). Note the data 
files do not contain geolocation information, the user needs to download the relevant TOMS 
data files for the given day. 

This product was previously available from the NASA National Space Science Data Center 
(NSSDC) under the name THIR Cloud Data TOMS Format (BCLT) with the identifier ESAD-00196 
(old id 78-098A-10E). 

1.1.1 The Temperature Humidity Infrared Radiometer 

The THIR instrument is a two channel high resolution scanning radiometer designed to perform 
two major functions: 

•  6.5 – 7.0 (6.7) micron channel gives information on the moisture content of the upper 
troposphere and stratosphere and the location of jet streams and frontal systems. The 
water vapor channel has a resolution of the sub-point is 20 km and operates mostly at 
night. 

•  10.5 – 12.5 (11.5) micron channel provides both day and night cloud top or surface 

temperatures. The ground resolution at the sub-point is 6.7 km and operates day and 
night. 

The optical system of the Nimbus THIR instrument consists of a scan mirror, a telescope, and a 
dichroic beam splitter. The scan mirror is inclined to 45 degrees to the axis of rotation (scans 
perpendicular to flight path) and the scan rate operation is 48 revolutions per minute. The field 

Page | 5  

 
 of view scans across the earth from east to west in daytime and west to east at night when 
traveling northward and southward respectively. A dichroic beam splitter divides the energy 
into two channels. A 20 milliradian channel detects energy in the 6.7 micron band while a 7.0 
milliradian channel detects energy in the 11.5 micron band. In both cases a germanium 
immersed thermistor bolometer is used. The swath width is about 2600 km 

The Nimbus-7 THIR instrument was basically the same as previous THIR flown on the Nimbus-4, 
5 and 6 satellites, except that the onboard system was digitized.  The experiment was 
successful returning data until 1985 when it was turned off to conserve spacecraft power. 

The principal investigator for the THIR experiment was Dr. Larry L. Stowe from NOAA NESDIS. 

1.1.2 Nimbus-7 Overview 

The Nimbus-7 satellite was successfully launched on October 24, 1978 and was the final in the 
Nimbus series. The spacecraft included nine experiments: (1) the Limb Infrared Monitor of the 
Stratosphere (LIMS) for making vertical profiles of temperature and concentrations of O3, H2O, 
NO2,  and  HNO3,  (2)  a  Stratospheric  and  Mesospheric  Sounder  (SAMS)  providing  vertical 
concentrations  of  H2O,  CH4,  CO  and  NO  and  measure  the  temperature  in  the  upper 
atmosphere,  (3)  the  Coastal-Zone  Color  Scanner  (CZCS)  for  mapping  ocean  chlorophyll 
II)  to  map  the 
concentrations,  (4)  the  Stratospheric  Aerosol  Measurement 
concentration  and  optical  properties  of  aerosols,  (5)  the  Earth  Radiation  Budget  (ERB)  for 
measuring  the  incoming  and  outgoing  reflected  and  emitted  radiation  of  the  Earth,  (6)  a 
Scanning  Multichannel  Microwave  Radiometer  (SMMR)  to  obtain  and  use  ocean  momentum 
and  energy-transfer  parameters  on  a  nearly  all-weather  operational  basis.,  (7)  a  Solar 
Backscatter  UV  (SBUV)  spectrometer  to  determine  the  vertical  distribution  of  ozone,  (8)  the 
Total  Ozone  Mapping  Spectrometer  (TOMS)  for  mapping  the  total  column  amount  of  ozone, 
and  (9)  the  Temperature  Humidity  Infrared  Radiometer  (THIR)  for  measuring  daytime  and 
nighttime surface and cloudtop temperatures, as well as the water vapor content of the upper 
atmosphere. 

II  (SAM 

The orbit of the satellite can be characterized by the following: 

  circular orbit at ~950 km 

  inclination of 99 degrees 

  period of an orbit is about 104 minutes 

  orbits cross the equator at 26 degrees of longitude separation 

  sun-synchronous 

Page | 6  

 
 
 
 
 1.2 Algorithm Background 

The  Nimbus-7  THIR  data  were  generated  from  the  spacecraft  telemetry,  attitude  and  orbital 
data.    The  data  were  originally  processed  on  IBM  360  computers  using  a  32-bit  architecture, 
and copied to 1600 bpi 9-track tapes for archival.  Further information on the THIR instrument 
and  data  processing  can  be  found  in  the  Nimbus-7  Users'  Guide  Section  9  and  the  Nimbus  7 
Temperature-Humidity Infrared Radiometer (THIR) Data User’s Guide. 

1.3 Data Disclaimer 

The data should be used with care and one should first read the Nimbus-7 User’s Guide, section 
9 describing the THIR experiment and section 7 describing the SBUV/TOMS experiment.  The 
data files contain no geolocation information. User’s will need to download the appropriate 
TOMS and SBUV orbit data files to geolocate these data. Users should cite this data product in 
their research. 

Page | 7  

 
 
 2. Data Organization 

The Nimbus-7 Temperature Humidity Infrared Radiometer Level-1 Cloud Data in TOMS Format 
(BCLT) spans the time period from October 31, 1978 to October 27, 1984. Each file contains one 
day (~14 orbits) of data. 

2.1 File Naming Convention 

The data product files are named according to the following convention: 

<Platform>-<Instrument>_<Product>_<Date>_<OrbitRange>-<TapeNumber>.<Suffix> 

where: 
o Platform = name of the platform or satellite (always Nimbus7) 
o) Instrument = name of the instrument and product (always THIR) 
o) Product = process level (L1) – and type (BCLT) 
o Date = Data start date and time in UTC in format <YYYY>m<MMDD>t<hhmmss> where 

1.  YYYY = 4 digit year (1978 – 1985) 
2.  MM = 2 digit month (01-12) 
3.  DD = 2 digit day of month (01-31) 

o OrbitRange = start - end orbit number when data were collected (preceded by the letter ‘o’) 
o TapeNumber = number of tape (preceeded by ‘DD’ - primary or ‘DC’ - backup) 
o Suffix = the file format (always TAP, indicating tape binary data) 

File name example: Nimbus7-THIR_L1-BCLT_1982m1001_o19871-19884_DD58729.TAP 

2.2 File Format and Structure 

The data are stored as they were originally written in IBM binary (big-endian) record oriented 
structured files. The files were written on the original 1600 bpi 9-track tapes using a blocked 
FORTRAN format each with a size of 8064 bytes (2016 words), each containing eight logical 
records of size 1016 bytes (252 words). A file typically contains a full day of data or 14 orbits. 

Each orbit starts with a header or documentation record (id = 30), followed by a set of TOMS 
and SBUV data records (id=31 and id=32 respectively), and finally a set of dummy records 
(id=33) to pad an eight record tape block. Within each orbit there are about 390 TOMS scans, 
each with 35 IFOV scenes per TOMS data record. Each orbit also contains about 96 SBUV IFOV 
scene per SBUV data record. For the contents and layout of the documentation, TOMS data, 
SBUV data, and dummy records, see section 3.1 

Page | 8  

 
 
 
 
 2.3 Key Science Data Fields 

The primary science data fields in this data product are the cloud amounts at low, mid and high 
altitudes, and average THIR radiances in units W/m2/sr at the 11.5 and 6.7 micron channels for 
each TOMS and SBUV IFOV. 

Figure 1: Typical content of a Nimbus 7 THIR BCLT data file. 

Page | 9  

 
 
 3. Data Contents 

The granularity of this data product is daily. 

3.1 Data Records 

The Nimbus-7 User’s Guide does not describe the layout of the file format. Refer to the older 
CLT file specification document Nimbus 7 THIR Data User’s Guide, Appendix F: Specification for 
the Clouds SBUV/TOMS Tape (CLT) Document No. T343041 (one for BCLT has not been located). 

Each data file contains multiple blocks, preceded and followed by a record length word with 
value 9504 specifying the block size in bytes. Each block contain 8 logical data records with 252 
four-byte words. A record identifier is found in the last 12 bits of the first word of each record.  
The first data record (record id = 30) is the documentation or header record for an orbit. This is 
followed by a series of TOMS data records (record id = 31) which holds 800 scans per orbit 
(though only less than 400 are used). This is then followed by a set of up to 25 SBUV data 
records (record id = 32) per orbit. Each orbit is filled with dummy records (record id = 33) to pad 
the block.

 3.2 Metadata 

The metadata are contained in a separate XML formatted file having the same name as the data 
file with .xml appended to it. 

Table 3-2: Metadata attributes associated with the data file.

 4. Reading the Data 

The data are written in a binary record-oriented format. Using the record format specification 
in the section above, users can write software to read the data files. Please note that the data 
were originally written using a big-endian format, therefore users on little-endian machines will 
need to swap bytes for the words. 

A sample FORTRAN program is included in the Appendix section which will read in the data 
records. Additionally a FORTRAN function is included to perform byte swapping. 

Page | 19  

 
 5. Data Services 

5.1 GES DISC Search 

The GES DISC provides a keyword, spatial, temporal and advanced (event) searches through its 
unified search and download interface: 

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/ 

5.2 Documentation 

The data product landing pages provide information about these data products, as well as links 
to download the data files and relevant documentation: 

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/datacollection/THIRN7L1BCLT_001.html 

5.3 Direct Download 

These data products are available for users to download directly using HTTPS: 

https://acdisc.gesdisc.eosdis.nasa.gov/data/Nimbus7_THIR_Level1/THIRN7L1BCLT.001/ 

Page | 20

C ^NAME: READ_BCLT 
C 
C ^MAJOR VARIABLES: 
C     FNAME  - name of input file 
C     BLOCK  - buffer for data block typically has eight records 
C     RECORD - buffer for holding logical data record 
C     WORD   - integer 4-byte word 
C     IOS    - I/O status number 
C 
C ^NOTES: 
C     Compile: gfortran -o READ_BCLT.EXE READ_BCLT.FOR 
C     See: Tape Specification for Clouds SBUV/TOMS Tape (CLT) T343041 
C 
C ^ORGANIZATION: NASA/GSFC, Code 610.2 
C 
C ^AUTHOR:  James Johnson 
C 
C ^ADDRESS: james.johnson@nasa.gov 
C 
C ^CREATED: July 25, 2017 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      CHARACTER          FNAME*1024 
      CHARACTER          BLOCK(8064)   ! Buffer = 8064 bytes 
      CHARACTER          RECORD(1008)  ! Buffer = 1008 bytes 
      CHARACTER          BUFF(4)       ! Buffer to hold 4-byte word 
      INTEGER*4          WORD          ! 4-byte word 
      INTEGER*4          IBLKSZ        ! Block size header 
      INTEGER*4          IRECSZ /1008/ ! Size of logical records 
      INTEGER*4          NRECS         ! Number of logical records 
      EQUIVALENCE        (BUFF, WORD) 

C     Get the name of the input data file to read 
      PRINT *, 'Enter the name of the input file:' 
      READ (5,'(A)') FNAME 

C     Open the specified input file 
      OPEN (UNIT=1, FILE=FNAME, STATUS='OLD', ACCESS='DIRECT', 
     &      FORM='UNFORMATTED', RECL=1, ERR=99, IOSTAT=IOS) 

C     Initialize N (block number) and IOFF (byte offset in file) 
      M=1 
      IOFF=0 

C     Loop through the file reading all blocks of data 
      DO 
C       Read the first 4-byte word or block size header 
        DO I=1,4 
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS, ERR=90) BUFF(I) 
        END DO 
        IBLKSZ = WORD 
        IOFF=IOFF+I-1 

Page | 23  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
         IF (IBLKSZ .EQ. 0) THEN 
C         PRINT '("WARNING: END-OF-TAPE MARK")' 
          GOTO 20 
        ENDIF 

C       Next read the block of data 
        DO I=1,IBLKSZ 
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS) BLOCK(I) 
          IF (IOS .NE. 0) THEN 
            PRINT '("ERROR: BLOCK ",I4,X,I4,", IOSTAT: ",I6)', N,I-1,IOS 
            IBLKSZ = I-1 
            GOTO 10 
          ENDIF 
        END DO 

        NRECS = IBLKSZ/IRECSZ 
   10   DO N=1,NRECS 
          RECORD = BLOCK((N-1)*IRECSZ+1:(N)*IRECSZ) 
C         Check the record type. This is byte 2 (3 unswapped) of first 
C         4-byte word. Value 30 = Header, 31 = TOMS, 32 = SBUV, 33 = Dummy 
          ITYPE = IAND(ICHAR(RECORD(3)), B'00111111') 
          IF (ITYPE .EQ. 30) THEN 
            CALL PRHREC(RECORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
          ELSE IF (ITYPE .EQ. 31) THEN 
            CALL PRTREC(RECORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
          ELSE IF (ITYPE .EQ. 32) THEN 
            CALL PRSREC(RECORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
          ELSE IF (ITYPE .EQ. 33) THEN 
            CALL PRXREC(RECORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
          ELSE 
            PRINT '("Unknown record type: ", I3)', ITYPE 
          ENDIF 
        END DO 
        IOFF=IOFF+I-1 

C       Finally read the last 4-byte word (should match first block size) 
   20   DO I=1,4 
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS, ERR=90) BUFF(I) 
        END DO 
        IF (IBLKSZ .NE. WORD) THEN 
          PRINT '("WARNING: IBLKSZ ",I10," != ",I10)', WORD, IBLKSZ 
        ENDIF 
        IOFF=IOFF+I-1 

        M=M+1 
      END DO 

C     Close the input file 
   90 CLOSE(1) 
      GOTO 100 

   99 PRINT '("ERROR: OPEN FILE, IOSTAT: ",I6)', IOS 

  100 STOP 
      END

 C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C ^SUBROUTINE: PRHREC 
C 
C     This Subroutine will Print the Documentation/Header Record 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      SUBROUTINE PRHREC(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

      INTEGER*4          WRDARR(252)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF        ! 4-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          I2BUFF(2)     ! 2-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          IRECNO        ! Physical Record Number 
      INTEGER*1          IRECID        ! Record Id 
      CHARACTER          SWPBYT*4      ! Function for swapping bytes 
      EQUIVALENCE        (BUFF, I4BUFF, I2BUFF) 

C     Physical Record Number and Record Id 
      PRINT '("***************")' 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(1) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      IRECNO = ISHFT(I2BUFF(2),-4) 
      PRINT '("RECNO  =",X,I6)', IRECNO 
      IRECID = ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,I3)', IRECID 

C     Orbit Number and Day of Year 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(2) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("ORBNUM =",X,I5)', I2BUFF(2)     ! SWAP ORDER 1 > 2 
      PRINT '("ORBDOY =",X,I5)', I2BUFF(1)     ! SWAP ORDER 2 > 1 

C     Year and Spare 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(3) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("ORBYR  =",X,I5)', I2BUFF(2)     ! SWAP ORDER 1 > 2 
C     PRINT '("SPARE  =",X,I5)', I2BUFF(1)     ! SWAP ORDER 2 > 1 

C     Orbit Start Time (sec) 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(4) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("ORBTS  =",I11)', I4BUFF 

C     Orbit End Time (sec) 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(5) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("ORBTE  =",I11)', I4BUFF 

Page | 25  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 C     SBUV First Scan Time (millisec) 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(6)   
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("SBUVT1 =",I11)', I4BUFF 

C     SBUV Last Scan Time (millisec) 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(7)  
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("SBUVTL =",I11)', I4BUFF 

C     TOMS First Scan Time (millisec) 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(8)   
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("TOMST1 =",I11)', I4BUFF 

C     TOMS Last Scan Time (millisec) 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(9)  
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("TOMSTL =",I11)', I4BUFF 

C     Spare (Words 10 - 252) 

      RETURN 
      END

 C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C ^SUBROUTINE: PRTREC 
C 
C     This Subroutine will Print the TOMS Data Record (Id=31) 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      SUBROUTINE PRTREC(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

      INTEGER*4          WRDARR(252)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF        ! 4-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          I2BUFF(2)     ! 2-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          IRECNO        ! Physical Record Number 
      INTEGER*1          IRECID        ! Record Id 
      CHARACTER          SWPBYT*4      ! Function for swapping bytes 
      EQUIVALENCE        (BUFF, I4BUFF, I2BUFF) 

C     Physical Record Number and Record Id 
      PRINT '("***************")' 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(1) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      IRECNO = ISHFT(I2BUFF(2),-4) 
      PRINT '("RECNO  =",X,I6)', IRECNO 
      IRECID = ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,I3)', IRECID 

C     Time at Start of Scan 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(2) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("TIME   =",I11)', I4BUFF 

C     Loop through 35 TOMS IFOVs. Radiances in units W/m2/sr and scaled 
      DO 10 I=1,35 
      PRINT '("--- IFOV ",I2, " ---")', I 
        I4BUFF = WRDARR(3+7*(I-1))  
        PRINT '("SRFCAT =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)) 
        PRINT '("POPSRF =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
        PRINT '("R11SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("R67SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),6) ! SCALED 0.015625 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(4+7*(I-1)) 
        PRINT '("RSFLOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("POPLOW =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
        PRINT '("R11LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("R67LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),6) ! SCALED 
0.015625 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(5+7*(I-1)) 
        PRINT '("RLOMID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("POPMID =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
        PRINT '("R11MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("R67MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),6) ! SCALED 
0.015625 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(6+7*(I-1)) 
        PRINT '("RMIDHI =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("POPHI  =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 

Page | 27  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
         PRINT '("R11HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),3) ! SCALED 0.125 
        PRINT '("R67HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),6) ! SCALED 
0.015625 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(7+7*(I-1)) 
        PRINT '("SPARE  =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)) 
        PRINT '("RCIRR  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),6) ! SCALED 
0.015625 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        PRINT '("THGHT  =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(1)                 ! SWAP ORDER 2>1 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(8+7*(I-1)) ! ALL SCALED 0.015625 (2^-6) 
        PRINT '("D11SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),6) 
        PRINT '("D11LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),6) 
        PRINT '("D11MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),6) 
        PRINT '("D11HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),6) 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(9+7*(I-1)) ! ALL SCALED 0.00392 (2^-8) 
        PRINT '("D67SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),8) 
        PRINT '("D67LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),8) 
        PRINT '("D67MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),8) 
        PRINT '("D67HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),8) 
   10 CONTINUE 
      PRINT '("---------------")' 

C     The rest of the bytes are spares and set to zero. 
C     The 2nd half of word 252 is last record flag 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(252) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("RECFLG =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(1)          ! SWAP ORDER 2>1 

      RETURN 
      END

 C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C ^SUBROUTINE: PRSREC 
C 
C     This Subroutine will Print the SBUV Data Record (Id=32) 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      SUBROUTINE PRSREC(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

      INTEGER*4          WRDARR(252)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF        ! 4-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          I2BUFF(2)     ! 2-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          IRECNO        ! Physical Record Number 
      INTEGER*1          IRECID        ! Record Id 
      CHARACTER          SWPBYT*4      ! Function for swapping bytes 
      EQUIVALENCE        (BUFF, I4BUFF, I2BUFF) 

C     Physical Record Number and Record Id 
      PRINT '("***************")' 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(1) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      IRECNO = ISHFT(I2BUFF(2),-4) 
      PRINT '("RECNO  =",X,I6)', IRECNO 
      IRECID = ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,I3)', IRECID 

C     Loop through up to 25 SBUV IFOVs, Radiances in units W/m2/sr and scaled 
      DO 10 I=1,25 
        I4BUFF = WRDARR(2+10*(I-1)) 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        IF (I4BUFF .EQ. 0) GOTO 10                           ! SKIP BLANK 
IFOV 
        PRINT '("-- IFOV: ",I2, " --")', I 
        PRINT '("TIME   =",I11)', I4BUFF 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(3+10*(I-1)) 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        PRINT '("POPSRF =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(2)                 ! SWAP ORDER 1>2 
        PRINT '("R11SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),3) ! SWAP ORDER 3>2 
        PRINT '("R67SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),6) ! SWAP ORDER 4>1 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(4+10*(I-1)) 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        PRINT '("POPLOW =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(2)                 ! SWAP ORDER 1>2 
        PRINT '("R11LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),3) ! SWAP ORDER 3>2 
        PRINT '("R67LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),6) ! SWAP ORDER 4>1 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(5+10*(I-1)) 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        PRINT '("POPMID =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(2)                 ! SWAP ORDER 1>2 
        PRINT '("R11MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),3) ! SWAP ORDER 3>2 
        PRINT '("R67MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),6) ! SWAP ORDER 4>1 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(6+10*(I-1)) 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        PRINT '("POPHI  =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(2)                 ! SWAP ORDER 1>2 
        PRINT '("R11HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),3) ! SWAP ORDER 3>2 

Page | 29  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
         PRINT '("R67HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),6) ! SWAP ORDER 4>1 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(7+10*(I-1)) 
        PRINT '("SPARE  =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)) 
        PRINT '("RCIRR  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),6) ! SCALED 2^-6 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
        PRINT '("THGHT  =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(1)                 ! SWAP ORDER 2>1 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(8+10*(I-1)) ! ALL SCALED 0.015625=2^-6 
        PRINT '("D11SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),6) 
        PRINT '("D11LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),6) 
        PRINT '("D11MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),6) 
        PRINT '("D11HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),6) 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(9+10*(I-1)) ! ALL SCALED 0.00392=2^-8 
        PRINT '("D67SRF =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)),8) 
        PRINT '("D67LOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),8) 
        PRINT '("D67MID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),8) 
        PRINT '("D67HI  =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),8) 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(10+10*(I-1)) 
        PRINT '("SRFCAT =",X,I3)', ICHAR(BUFF(1:1)) 
        PRINT '("RSFLOW =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)),3) ! SCALED 0.125=2^-
3 
        PRINT '("RLOMID =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(3:3)),3) ! SCALED 0.125=2^-
3 
        PRINT '("RMIDHI =",X,I6)', ISHFT(ICHAR(BUFF(4:4)),3) ! SCALED 0.125=2^-
3 

        I4BUFF = WRDARR(11+10*(I-1)) 
        BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4)  
        PRINT '("TMTHIR =",I11)', I4BUFF 
   10 CONTINUE                           
      PRINT '("--------------")'         

C     The 1st half of word 252 is spare bytes set to zero 
C     The 2nd half of word 252 is last record flag 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(252) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("RECFLG =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(1)          ! SWAP ORDER 2 > 1 

      RETURN 
      END

 C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C ^SUBROUTINE: PRXREC 
C 
C     This Subroutine will Print the Dummy Record (Id=33) 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      SUBROUTINE PRXREC(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

      INTEGER*4          WRDARR(252)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF        ! 4-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          I2BUFF(2)     ! 2-byte integer buffer 
      INTEGER*2          IRECNO        ! Physical Record Number 
      INTEGER*1          IRECID        ! Record Id 
      CHARACTER          SWPBYT*4      ! Function for swapping bytes 
      EQUIVALENCE        (BUFF, I4BUFF, I2BUFF) 

      PRINT '("***************")' 

C     Physical Record Number and Record Id 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(1)                   
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4)          
      IRECNO = ISHFT(I2BUFF(2),-4)         
      PRINT '("RECNO  =",X,I6)', IRECNO 
      IRECID = ICHAR(BUFF(2:2)) 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,I3)', IRECID 

C     The rest of the bytes are spares and set to zero. 
C     The 2nd half of word 252 is last record flag 
      I4BUFF = WRDARR(252) 
      BUFF = SWPBYT(BUFF(1:4), 4) 
      PRINT '("RECFLG =",X,I6)', I2BUFF(1)          ! SWAP ORDER 2 > 1 

      RETURN 
      END 

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C ^FUNCTION: SWPBYT 
C 
C     This function will swap the bytes of a data element 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      CHARACTER*4 FUNCTION SWPBYT(DATBUF, NBYTES) 

      CHARACTER          DATBUF(4)      ! Input data buffer 
C     CHARACTER          DATBUF*4       ! Input data buffer 

      DO 10 K=1,NBYTES 
        SWPBYT(K:K) = DATBUF(NBYTES-K+1) 
C       SWPBYT(K:K) = DATBUF(NBYTES-K+1:NBYTES-K+1) 
   10 CONTINUE 

      RETURN 
      END
